author: @handleismyhandle
text: I was just looking at tbe prices of 316L stainless sheet, and given that the plates won't last forever regardless, it has me thinking about the fact that I can pull pieces of charcoal out of my woodstove that will register less than an ohm of resistance across them. The trick is to get the fire very hot by blowing on it. Years ago I wanted to use a microwave to further the graphitization, and I found that it worked, but for some strange reason I got hung up on the idea of creating a vacuum inside of it, vs just using a simple kiln. Graphite is a susceptor material, so it will get especially hot in a microwave. So, the idea then is to make the charcoal almost as conductive as metal, which will work with a microwave kiln for sure, and then find a way to consolidate the resulting material into conductive sheets by grinding them and perhaps binding them with something that is insoluble in water? There has to be a way to get sufficient conductivity. . . Maybe this sort of carbon could be used to make all of the neutral plates without actually being so conductive? I am not really sure how the voltage manages to spread evenly between the plates, being that they are not physically connected to anything except the electrolyte, but evidently it does. If I manage to figure this one out I will try to share the results somehow. DIY carbon or flake graphite in a slightly less conductive form due to PVA or whatever admixture might not work for the end plates, but the neutral ones? An idea for people to try, anyway.

author: @thequixotryworkshop2424
text: Happy Hydrogen day.. What do hydrogen's atomic weight and October 8 have in common? Both share numerical digits—1.008 for the atomic weight of element number one in the periodic table, and 10/08 for the date. This is why October 8, chosen as Hydrogen and Fuel Cell Day, marks a symbolic opportunity every year to celebrate hydrogen and to talk about the role it can play as we transition to a cleaner and more equitable energy future.

author: @Atimar01
text:
small hint though ...
you use the "dry cell" as an ordenary cell ...
normally the fluid container in this setup gets both in and outlet and sits above the cell
a normal cell has to be refilled above the plate level but because of the disighn, the dry cell (dry on the outside) always has maximum plate coverage with electrolite since the containeris above.
also the output improves since the electrolite is constantely pumped through so the plates keep free of bubbles faster so current is more efficient per cm2.
just put a tight lid on the electrolite container with a tube sticking out and  feed that to the bubbler. 
once you do a liter per minute per watt calculation you'll see why ;)

author: @davidprock904
text:
Got some questions and ideas 💡, 
The reason for any kind of electrolyte is just to make the water conductive, and that's it?...
So can doing this replace using electrolyte...
The two opposing surface areas where the water breaks down, Coat them with a super Hydrophilic coating, creating the Exclusion Zone (EZ Water), it becomes evenly negatively charged, and the positive charges  pushed out are the strongest against the negatively structured layer and get weaker the further away up to where its just neutral water,. Put them close enough to where the positive layer from each surface overlap, but not to close to where the EZ layers become one. Now you have a conductive path of water between your electrodes! 
Will that replace using electrolyte?????
If so, and even if not I have an ultimate design for a cell combining different technologies in a very unique way. Its more than im willing to type out at the moment. 
But could we have an audio chat about it, like on Skype etc?

author: @kennethbarli4469
text:
Use 988 parts of distilled water 2 parts of caustic soda. (Sodium hydroxide)
Have 1.2 millimeters between the plates.
There should be 3 volts between each plate.
Focus on how much gas you produce compared to amps.
Amps alone, says nothing.
It may disappear in heat.

author: @guy339
text: I like this method of generating HHO. HHO is facinating stuff and I no longer believe the conspiracy theories as to why we all are not just using this for our heating and electrical power needs and desires. My own experiences with HHO suggest its reaction speed when imploded is far to fast to be practical on a larger than novelty application. In a nutshell its like trying to control a stallion with a wet noodle. when you try and do anything useful with it on a simple man's budget.  I can personally attest to what a hole through a ceiling  looks like when this HHO implodes.  I'm sure It can be useful if you have the $jack to tame it. In all fairness before you call me a troll try to make HHO "useful" yourself without spending a small fortune.  I spent the time and meager funds in my attempts.  I left the hole in my ceiling as a reminder of how my desire to save money can cost more than installing a simple wood burning stove. I throw the gauntlet down for Mr. Murray-Smith to prove me wrong.  Please show me the path to making this stuff useful sir.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh no mate I agree - it is hard stuff to tame - my though was along the lines of separation and on demand volume control rather than bulk production and storage - but I am a step by step kind of guy and I hate spending money lol

author: @PressToDigitate
text:
(Psssst:  Check my comment on yesterday's Ceramic Supercapacitor video).
When you substitute an Aluminum Anode for the Power Supply - so that the Cell produces DC Current while making Hydrogen instead of consuming it - note that Sodium Hydroxide will function as a true catalyst, fully regenerating as it operates, while Potassium Hydroxide will not.
Also: Just as you use that little electric motor to demonstrate the useful power being output by your batteries and supercapacitors, you might consider an RC Model Aircraft Engine that will run on Hydrogen or HHO to demonstrate the useful fuel being output by your electrolyzers and [reverse] fuel cells.

author: @janbeute4902
text:
To break down one urea molecule and release the two molecules of hydrogen gas they applied 0.37 V across the cell. This is much lower than the 1.23 V needed to do the same to water.
Carbon dioxide is  generated during the reaction of urea, but it is not released as a gas and instead reacts with the potassium hydroxide in the electrolyte to form potassium carbonate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's partially true mate - in practice the applied voltage is 0.447 with a nickel catalyst - the electro-oxidation of urea is fairly complex particularly with catalysts and dependent on pH

author: @Barskor1
text: Piston engines running HHO or anything really no one asked but if you are going to make one go horizontal with the combustion and expansion chamber use a rack and pinion system as the kinetic energy converter and then at the end of the power stroke at the far side you have another combustion chamber that then drives the piston back with the rack disengaged or engaging a different pinion probably a second drive shaft pinon with gearing to keep everything moving in the same direction for the drive shaft now with a 4 chamber system working the one rack and pinion converter you can have opposite sides firing at the same time so that the top and bottom of one pion are being driven.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have been looking at small engines as that is in my mind mate

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Wonderful so what do you think of the idea?

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Another idea is essentially a water wheel driven by explosions from symmetrically placed angled side chambers sequentially fired around a circular tube track the paddles are piston heads and also sweep out the exhaust through valved ports, speed of the engine is controlled by how much HHO is let in at a time and the timing of ignitions.
The tube would have a thin slot on the inner edge for the piston wheel disk and the disk would have a ridge and wiping seal for containment.

author: @IrishSkruffles
text:
Hi Rob,
You probably know this but for these so called "dry cells" you want to put the gas output pipe back into the water reservoir. This way it does not matter where the water level is in the reservoir/tank, you can even mount the tank well above the cell if you want.
The gas output line will now put out both gas and the water (electrolyte) so you  take the actual gas output from the top of the water (electrolyte) reservoir/tank, through at least a water bubbler (flashback arrestor is a nice thing to have also)

This does require a sealed reservoir/tank, but in my opinion it is a good setup. Keep up the good work, sorry for the essay lol!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I do mate - I am just making this as simple as possible for the experiments - but your comments are spot on and very good advice

author: @victoryfirst2878
text: Am impressed with your progress of the cell. I remember my science teacher saying that the best voltage for electrolysis was 18 volts. Have you found out that this is correct ?? Or does 12 volts really do the trick ??? Keep on making great videos Bob. Thanks VF

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it depends on what you are electrolysing mate - water takes at least 1.23 volts urea is 0.47 volts. There is resistance in the cell so the applied voltage is always going to be higher but not too much - if the cell gets hot then all you are really doing is boiling the water and producing steam - so like so many things it's a balance - the reason we are applying 12 volts is we have 6 cells in each unit so the average voltage per cell is 2 volts even though the unit takes 12 volts - hope this helps

author: @rbouakaz
text: This is great! But the problem is that it requires energy to create hydrogen. So it makes sence to use this system as a battery for energy generated from wind or sun. But then comes the problem with storage of hydrogen. How do you compress hydrogen in a safe and smart way? And how can you the produce energy from that stored hydrogen? Fuel cell ? Stirling engine? There are some videos on youtube - but hope to se your take on it? Kind Regards :) and thank you for the channel!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it requires energy to create any fuel mate - the hydrogen generated from urea electrolysis is net positive for energy. That's not because you are magically creating energy it is only because the bond energy in urea is lower than the bond energy in water - so it takes less energy to split urea than you get when you burn the hydrogen into water

author: @karlmyers6518
text: Another great video. Currently building my magnetic stirrer hot plate. Running dc motors but I can't seem to add capacitor without making a manual switch. Any help appreciated

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: not sure why you want to do that for a stirrer mate most dc motors that size don't need one

replies.author: @karlmyers6518
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkeringthat's what I was thinking.  The motor doesn't start without a little  push it's a 14.4 volt dc motor.  I took out of a robotic Hoover.  I got 5 motors and thought it would be perfect but I'm scratching my head lol.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @karlmyers6518 sounds like a squirrel cage motor mate - you need to identify the aux winding if that is the case and bridge the cap over that - you probably need a disconnect switch too as they don't usually like the cap being inline when running - but as I don't know this motor I am really just guessing

replies.author: @karlmyers6518
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering After all that it looks like a fault in the power supply under load! I've gone out and borrowed my car battery and it spun straight up. New power supply needed. (New volt meter in the post)  I'll be sure to upload some pictures when it's done. Cheers mate

replies.author: @IrishSkruffles
replies.text:
It would run, but it won't run well or for very long. HHO gas burns extremely hot and fast, and all engines rely on a bit of extra petrol to keep the fuel/air mix rich so that the engine parts don't get too hot.

HHO burns very fast like I said, so it's very possible for it to ignite and go past the valves before they fully close.. that could lead to a very big bang indeed

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
Seems to be producing pretty well, for a quick ballpark figure of production volume / time, run your output into an upturned water-filled test tube, time how long it takes to fill.  But you know that :)
Although your cell is a pretty big advance in terms of production simplicity and cost, I can't see this particular setup being overly useful for urea electrolysis, - although you're getting more H2 than you would with a "classic" dry cell, the hydrogen is mixed with nitrogen rather than being a directly usable 2H2 + O2 feed.
